title: With no time to escape, workers braced for slide
summary: With no time to escape, workers braced for slide By NATALIE PHILLIPS and CRAIG MEDRED Daily News reporters Larry Bushnell saw it coming. Just seconds before another avalanche ripped down on the Seward Highway on Tuesday afternoon, a spotter radioed him and two other state bulldozer operators working in the Bird Flats area. The message: Get out and get out now. But how do you prepare for an avalanche blast with only a few seconds warning? There weren't many choices, Bushnell recalled later. All three spun their bulldozers to face the mountain and lifted their blades in the air to absorb the brunt of the avalanche. "I could hear the windows starting to crackle and shatter," Bushnell said. "Then one popped and so did the others. There was so much pressure." Bushnell, who works for the state D…

title: Storm losses top $16 million
summary: Storm losses top $16 million Knowles requests federal aid By ELIZABETH MANNING Daily News reporter Gov. Tony Knowles on Monday estimated that Southcentral Alaska's recent storms and avalanches have cost at least $16 million, including damage to property and the cost of clearing roads and repairing utilities. Speaking from a stage set on top of a low-boy trailer behind a state maintenance garage, Knowles also recognized state employees who worked long hours during the series of storms and subsequent avalanches. "It's been a difficult time for Alaskans," Knowles said. "But Alaskans came through in pretty good shape." Southcentral was hit with a series of storms that began on Dec. 21, unleashing at least 33 avalanches. The slides, combined with rain, hurricane-force winds, record snowfall, ic…

title: 2 men jailed in slaying
summary: 2 men jailed in slaying Ex-worker shot boss, police say By KAREN AHO Daily News reporter :A week after quitting their jobs in anger, two former Wendy's employees talked their way into the closed restaurant early Tuesday and shot a former boss in the head after forcing her to open the safe, Anchorage police said Wednesday. Information from a restaurant worker inexplicably spared by the robbers enabled police to arrest the former employees for the murder of Dianne J. Rostron, 31, within 27 hours of the shooting, police said. Omelette Muasau and Jeremy Sean Palmer, both 18, were in Cook Inlet Pre-Trial Facility, each on $500,000 cash bail. Police Sgt. Ken Spadafora said he believes Muasau intended to kill Rostron when he and Palmer knocked on the glass doors of the fast-food chain's Midtown b…

title: INS ends woman's jail ordeal
summary: INS ends woman's jail ordeal By LISA DEMER Daily News reporter Anchorage resident Hawa Said served her time on a felony drug conviction in May, but she didn't get out of prison. In a nationally publicized case, immigration officials kept the 20-year-old pregnant woman confined without bail for the next six months. The U.S. Immigration and Naturalization Service wanted to deport her. U.S. immigration law is hard-line on noncitizens convicted of serious crimes. They must be detained after their criminal case is resolved and are often sent back to wherever they came from. But Said's attorney contended she is a U.S. citizen and fought to have her freed. "This is a classic case of the government just doing something that makes no sense," attorney Margaret Stock said before the hearing. The comp…

title: Young delivers oil-revenue bill
summary: Young delivers oil-revenue bill By DAVID WHITNEY Daily News Washington Bureau WASHINGTON - Landmark legislation dedicating federal offshore oil revenues to conservation and wildlife cleared a crucial step Wednesday when it won approval by the House Resources Committee. The vote followed a four-hour committee meeting in which conservative Western Republicans tried repeatedly to weaken the bill, which they regard as an assault on private property rights. But the panel's Republican chairman, Alaska Rep. Don Young, gathered enough Republican backers to join united Democrats in beating back more than a dozen amendments. In the final 37 to 12 vote, only 13 of 25 Republicans supported the measure. It was Young's finest hour since becoming chairman of the committee after Republicans took over the …

title: Beanie broker brings out animal in angry buyers
summary: Beanie broker brings out animal in angry buyers The Associated Press FAIRBANKS - Robert Easterbrooks swears it was all just a big mistake that put an e-mail posse of angry Beanie Baby collectors on his trail. But refund checks seem to have appeased those who won his Internet auctions and sent money orders for the stuffed creatures. "I was not aware of what was going on," said Easterbrooks, who was driving from Fairbanks to California when mayhem erupted over his failure to deliver dozens of Beanie Babies sold on the Internet auction site eBay. "This whole thing, as far as I'm concerned, is somebody jumping the gun because I lost e-mail." The roots of the trouble are embedded deep in Beanie Baby mania. As if the urge felt by a collector to acquire the Ty Inc. toys as fast as they are retire…
